tagged_story_for_tts:
Microcracks as shields.<[silence:40ms]> Collagen as shock absorber.<[silence:40ms]>

The term bone comes from Old English bān, meaning "the hard tissue forming the skeleton"—but hardness alone would make bones brittle, like chalk.<[silence:40ms]> Bones resist breaking because they’re built like reinforced concrete: stiff minerals (hydroxyapatite crystals) embedded in a flexible protein mesh (collagen fibers).<[silence:40ms]> When force hits, collagen stretches like a bungee cord, absorbing energy before it snaps.<[silence:40ms]> Microcracks form first—not failures, but stress distributors, redirecting pressure like a river splitting around rocks.<[silence:40ms]> A deer’s leg bone, for example, can endure 150 megapascals of pressure (twice a car tire’s max) because its spiral collagen layout twists under load, turning a sharp impact into a harmless vibration.<[silence:40ms]>

Three related subjects are self-healing materials, biomimicry in engineering, and how tendons store elastic energy.<[silence:40ms]>
clean_story:
Microcracks as shields. Collagen as shock absorber.

The term bone comes from Old English bān, meaning "the hard tissue forming the skeleton"—but hardness alone would make bones brittle, like chalk. Bones resist breaking because they’re built like reinforced concrete: stiff minerals (hydroxyapatite crystals) embedded in a flexible protein mesh (collagen fibers). When force hits, collagen stretches like a bungee cord, absorbing energy before it snaps. Microcracks form first—not failures, but stress distributors, redirecting pressure like a river splitting around rocks. A deer’s leg bone, for example, can endure 150 megapascals of pressure (twice a car tire’s max) because its spiral collagen layout twists under load, turning a sharp impact into a harmless vibration.

Three related subjects are self-healing materials, biomimicry in engineering, and how tendons store elastic energy.
